refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication reliability within the customer-defined packet loss threshold during agreed network conditions.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation capability and (2) meeting a specific quality metric (packet loss limit). The refined FR isolates the quality/reliability obligation as a single atomic requirement in the problem domain. It removes implementation bias ('communication quality') in favor of the measurable outcome ('packet loss threshold'). The phrase 'during agreed network conditions' acknowledges the boundary condition without prescribing how it is achieved. This formulation is solution-neutral: it does not mandate redundancy, error correction, protocol selection, or network architecture—only the observable outcome.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation trials under agreed network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profile) and measure end-to-end packet loss rate. Confirm that measured packet loss does not exceed the customer-defined threshold (e.g., ≤1%). Log packet statistics at the application layer to distinguish transport-level loss from application-level loss if needed.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall enable remote operation of heavy equipment from a tele-operator station.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation command and feedback transmission with packet loss not exceeding the customer-defined limit under agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time network quality metrics (latency, jitter, loss rate) to maintain effective throughput within acceptable operator response time.
DP2: Establish a dual-path communication architecture with independent network routes and automatic failover logic to ensure continuous command/feedback exchange during transient network degradation.

DP3: Deploy application-level forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy ratios, tuned to customer-defined packet loss threshold and agreed network conditions, to recover lost packets without retransmission.

DP4: Implement a buffering and prioritization strategy that segregates critical control commands from non-critical telemetry, ensuring safety-critical packets are delivered within latency bounds even under packet loss.

DP5: Establish a closed-loop network health monitoring and operator notification system that measures real-time packet loss against the customer-defined threshold and alerts operators when conditions degrade beyond acceptable limits.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3 + DP4 + DP5 (integrated approach): Deploy application-level forward error correction with adaptive redundancy tuning to the customer-defined packet loss threshold; segregate and prioritize safety-critical control packets from telemetry; establish continuous network health monitoring with operator-facing alerts and threshold-based operational constraints. This combination decouples the reliability mechanism (FEC) from transport-layer specifics, allows graceful degradation under varying network conditions, and provides transparency into whether agreed conditions are being met.

potential_sources: DP3 (FEC) and DP4 (prioritization) are logically independent: FEC operates on encoded packet streams; prioritization operates on packet classification and queue discipline. Both can be implemented and tuned separately.

DP5 (monitoring) is loosely coupled to DP3 and DP4: it observes their outputs (packet loss rate, latency) but does not dictate their internal logic. Monitoring can be added or modified without redesigning FEC or prioritization.

Risk: If FEC redundancy ratio is not tuned to match the customer-defined threshold and actual network conditions, the system may over-provision (wasting bandwidth) or under-provision (failing to meet reliability target). Mitigation: DP5 must include a feedback loop to validate and adjust FEC parameters post-deployment.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP avoids naming specific protocols (e.g., TCP, UDP, QUIC) or vendor solutions, remaining solution-neutral. It decomposes reliability into three orthogonal concerns: error correction, packet prioritization, and observability. This supports independent module ownership and testing.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: 'Agreed network conditions' are formally defined by the customer (e.g., maximum latency, typical jitter, baseline packet loss rate under normal operation). Risk: If conditions are vague or change post-deployment, the FEC tuning and threshold monitoring become misaligned. Mitigation: Require explicit network SLA documentation before design finalization.

Assumption: The teleoperation application can tolerate the latency introduced by FEC encoding/decoding and buffering. Risk: If operator response time is extremely tight (e.g., <50 ms round-trip), FEC overhead may violate safety requirements. Mitigation: Conduct latency budget analysis early; consider hybrid approaches (FEC for non-critical telemetry, retransmission for critical commands).

Assumption: The customer-defined packet loss threshold (≤1%) is achievable under the agreed network conditions with reasonable redundancy overhead. Risk: If the actual network is worse than assumed, no DP can guarantee the threshold without excessive redundancy or operational constraints. Mitigation: Include a pre-deployment network characterization phase and establish fallback operational modes (e.g., reduced autonomy, operator-in-the-loop confirmation).

Assumption: Operator notification and manual intervention (DP5 alerts) are acceptable as part of the reliability strategy. Risk: If the system must be fully autonomous and self-healing, DP5 alone is insufficient; additional automated recovery logic (e.g., DP2 failover) may be required. Mitigation: Clarify operational autonomy requirements with the customer.

Assumption: The worksite safety plan and operational policies do not mandate specific communication technologies or protocols. Risk: If regulations require certified protocols (e.g., industrial wireless standards), the solution-neutral DP may need to be constrained. Mitigation: Review safety plan and policies early; document any technology mandates.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine the FR to explicitly state: (1) the customer-defined packet loss threshold value (e.g., ≤1%); (2) the agreed network conditions (latency range, jitter, baseline loss rate, duration of acceptable degradation); (3) the maximum acceptable latency for operator feedback (round-trip time budget); (4) whether the system must be fully autonomous or operator-supervised during network degradation. This will enable DP tuning parameters (FEC redundancy ratio, buffer sizes, alert thresholds) to be derived deterministically rather than empirically. Additionally, add a secondary FR addressing the observability requirement: 'The NGHE solution shall provide real-time visibility into network health and reliability metrics to enable operators and maintainers to assess whether agreed conditions are being met and to trigger manual intervention or operational constraints when thresholds are exceeded.' This elevates DP5 from a supporting mechanism to an explicit functional requirement, ensuring it receives appropriate design rigor and testing.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates network reliability mechanisms (FEC, adaptive redundancy), packet prioritization logic (safety vs. telemetry segregation), continuous network health monitoring, and operator-facing alerts. Owns the decision logic for operational constraints based on network state.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface to underlying transport layer; Teleoperation module configures FEC parameters and redundancy tuning on Network Link; receives raw packet loss metrics and link health telemetry.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module emits network health telemetry (packet loss rate, FEC overhead, adaptive redundancy state) to monitoring and logging systems.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module triggers SafetyAlert when network conditions degrade below agreed thresholds or when safety-critical packet delivery cannot be guaranteed.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator commands and applies packet prioritization logic; safety-critical tasking is segregated and prioritized over telemetry.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Teleoperation is the natural owner of network reliability and operator-facing constraints. FEC, redundancy tuning, and packet prioritization are core to remote operation. Network health monitoring and alerts align with operator situational awareness. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert interface requires coordination with Safety Services for threshold enforcement, but decision logic remains in Teleoperation.

Moderate fit but misaligned. Safety Services would own safety-critical packet prioritization and alert generation, but FEC and adaptive redundancy tuning are not safety-specific concerns—they are operational reliability mechanisms. Placing the entire DP here would couple safety logic to network transport details, creating unnecessary diffusion. Safety Services should consume SafetyAlert output, not own the network health monitoring.

Poor fit. Vehicle module is the remote asset being controlled; it should not own teleoperation network reliability logic. FEC, redundancy tuning, and operator alerts are teleoperation-side concerns. Placing DP here would create tight coupling between vehicle control and network transport, violating separation of concerns.

Weak fit. NGHE Solution is likely a top-level system container. While it could theoretically host Teleoperation as a subsystem, assigning DP directly to NGHE Solution would be overly broad and would obscure ownership. Better to assign to the specific Teleoperation module within NGHE Solution.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of network reliability mechanisms because: (1) it is responsible for remote operation quality and operator situational awareness; (2) FEC and adaptive redundancy are operational concerns, not vehicle-side concerns; (3) packet prioritization (safety vs. telemetry) is a teleoperation-layer decision; (4) operator-facing alerts and threshold-based constraints are teleoperation responsibilities. The DP is cohesive within Teleoperation and diffusion is minimal (only SafetyAlert coordination with Safety Services, which is a clean interface boundary).
containment_risks: SafetyAlert interface requires tight coordination with Safety Services to ensure that network-induced operational constraints do not conflict with safety-critical requirements. If Safety Services has independent safety thresholds, there is a risk of conflicting constraint enforcement.

Adaptive redundancy tuning requires knowledge of customer-defined packet loss thresholds, which may be stored or managed outside Teleoperation. If threshold configuration is owned by a separate module, Teleoperation must receive thresholds via a well-defined interface (e.g., configuration input on Network Link or a new Tasking parameter).

Network Link interface must expose sufficient telemetry (packet loss rate, link quality metrics) for Teleoperation to make informed FEC and redundancy decisions. If Network Link does not provide this visibility, Teleoperation cannot implement continuous health monitoring.
Operator-facing alerts (SafetyAlert) must be delivered reliably even under degraded network conditions. If SafetyAlert itself uses the same unreliable network, there is a circular dependency risk.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP is necessary. The selected DP (DP3 + DP4 + DP5) is well-suited to Teleoperation ownership. However, clarify the following to strengthen containment: (1) Ensure Network Link interface exposes packet loss metrics and link health telemetry; (2) Define how customer-defined packet loss thresholds are provided to Teleoperation (e.g., via configuration or Tasking parameter); (3) Establish a clear SafetyAlert contract with Safety Services to avoid conflicting constraint enforcement; (4) Confirm that SafetyAlert delivery mechanism is independent of the primary teleoperation network link to avoid circular reliability dependencies.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 + DP4 + DP5 (integrated approach) directly addresses the FR requirement to maintain teleoperation communication reliability within customer-defined packet loss threshold. The combination of application-level FEC with adaptive redundancy tuning (DP3), safety-critical packet segregation and prioritization (DP4), and continuous network health monitoring with operator-facing alerts (DP5) provides a complete logical solution. The DP decouples reliability mechanisms from transport-layer specifics and enables graceful degradation, which is appropriate for the stated problem. Acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% or customer-defined target) is directly measurable against the DP outputs.

diffusion_risks: SafetyAlert interface creates a coordination dependency with Safety Services. If Safety Services enforces independent safety thresholds that conflict with network-induced operational constraints, constraint enforcement logic may become diffused across two modules. Mitigation: establish a clear SafetyAlert contract defining which module owns threshold enforcement decision logic.

Adaptive redundancy tuning requires access to customer-defined packet loss thresholds. If threshold configuration is managed outside Teleoperation (e.g., in a separate Configuration or Policy module), Teleoperation must receive thresholds via a well-defined input interface. Risk of diffusion if threshold logic is scattered across multiple modules. Mitigation: define a single authoritative source for threshold configuration and expose it to Teleoperation via explicit interface.

Network Link interface must expose packet loss metrics and link health telemetry for continuous monitoring. If Network Link does not provide sufficient visibility, Teleoperation cannot implement the monitoring logic, forcing diffusion of health monitoring responsibility. Mitigation: explicitly specify Network Link interface contract to include packet loss rate, FEC overhead, and link quality metrics.

SafetyAlert delivery mechanism must be independent of the primary teleoperation network link to avoid circular reliability dependency. If SafetyAlert is routed through the same unreliable link, alert delivery cannot be guaranteed under degraded conditions. Mitigation: confirm SafetyAlert uses a separate, more reliable transport or out-of-band mechanism.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. The DP is cohesive within Teleoperation ownership: FEC, adaptive redundancy tuning, and packet prioritization are core teleoperation-layer concerns; operator-facing alerts and threshold-based constraints align with operator situational awareness responsibilities. The module boundary is well-defined and external interfaces are explicit. Minor diffusion is limited to SafetyAlert coordination with Safety Services, which is a clean interface boundary rather than scattered responsibility. Placement in Safety Services (0.65 containment) or Vehicle (0.4 containment) would create inappropriate coupling of network transport details to safety or vehicle control logic, violating separation of concerns. Teleoperation is the correct owner.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The Teleoperation module owns all mechanisms required to verify the FR acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% under agreed conditions). Continuous network health monitoring (DP5) provides real-time packet loss metrics. FEC and adaptive redundancy tuning (DP3) are directly observable and tunable. Operator-facing alerts (DP5) provide transparency into whether agreed conditions are being met. Verification can be performed via: (1) instrumentation of packet loss rate from Network Link interface; (2) measurement of FEC overhead and redundancy state; (3) validation of alert triggering logic against threshold criteria; (4) end-to-end testing under simulated network conditions matching customer-defined thresholds.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to a single, well-scoped IM (Teleoperation) with explicit ownership of all DP components. Interfaces to external modules (Network Link, SafetyAlert, Telemetry, Tasking) are defined with direction and purpose. No ambiguity exists regarding which module is responsible for reliability mechanisms, packet prioritization, health monitoring, or alert generation. Allocation supports traceability from FR through DP to IM and enables clear ownership for implementation, testing, and operational support.

recommended_revisions: Strengthen Network Link interface contract: explicitly specify that Network Link must expose packet loss rate, FEC overhead, link quality metrics (e.g., latency, jitter), and link state (healthy/degraded/failed) to enable Teleoperation continuous health monitoring. Document the telemetry schema and update frequency.

Define threshold configuration interface: clarify how customer-defined packet loss thresholds are provided to Teleoperation. Options: (1) configuration parameter loaded at initialization; (2) dynamic parameter via Tasking interface; (3) separate Configuration module with read-only interface to Teleoperation. Choose one and document the interface contract.

Establish SafetyAlert contract with Safety Services: define the SafetyAlert interface semantics, including alert types (e.g., 'network_degraded', 'safety_critical_delivery_at_risk'), threshold criteria, and expected Safety Services response. Clarify that Teleoperation owns network health decision logic and Safety Services owns safety-critical response logic to avoid diffusion of constraint enforcement.

Confirm SafetyAlert delivery independence: verify that SafetyAlert mechanism (e.g., out-of-band channel, dedicated safety link, or local alert) is independent of the primary teleoperation network link. Document the SafetyAlert transport mechanism and its reliability guarantees.

Add interface for operational constraint feedback: define how Teleoperation communicates network-induced operational constraints (e.g., 'telemetry-only mode', 'reduced command frequency') back to the operator or Tasking module. This ensures operator awareness of degraded conditions and prevents unsafe command issuance.

Document FEC and redundancy tuning algorithm: specify the adaptive logic that tunes FEC parameters and redundancy level based on observed packet loss rate. Include decision criteria, tuning bounds, and convergence behavior to ensure predictable and verifiable behavior under varying network conditions.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, indicating the decomposition is logically sound and architecturally appropriate. However, the recommended_revisions identify six critical interface and contract specifications that must be formalized before proceeding to sub-level decomposition. Given the high safety criticality of this teleoperation system, these revisions are not optional refinements—they are essential preconditions for downstream implementation and verification. Specifically: (1) Network Link interface contract must explicitly specify packet loss metrics and link health telemetry schema to prevent diffusion of monitoring responsibility; (2) threshold configuration interface must establish a single authoritative source to avoid scattered constraint logic; (3) SafetyAlert contract must clarify ownership boundaries between Teleoperation (network health decision) and Safety Services (safety response) to prevent diffusion of constraint enforcement; (4) SafetyAlert delivery independence must be confirmed to avoid circular reliability dependencies; (5) operational constraint feedback interface must be defined to ensure operator situational awareness under degraded conditions; (6) FEC and adaptive redundancy tuning algorithm must be documented to ensure predictable and verifiable behavior. These revisions strengthen the DP specification and IM interface contracts without changing the fundamental decomposition structure. Once revisions are incorporated, the FR-DP-IM chain will be sufficiently detailed to support sub-level decomposition with high confidence.

revise.why: The DP specification (DP3 + DP4 + DP5) is logically correct but insufficiently detailed in its interface contracts and algorithmic behavior. Revisions must address: (1) DP3 (adaptive redundancy tuning) requires explicit specification of the tuning algorithm, decision criteria, and bounds; (2) DP4 (packet prioritization and segregation) requires clarification of how safety-critical packets are identified and how constraint feedback is communicated to upstream modules; (3) DP5 (continuous monitoring and alerts) requires formalization of the Network Link interface contract (metrics, schema, update frequency) and SafetyAlert contract (alert types, threshold criteria, delivery mechanism independence). These revisions are DP-level concerns because they define the logical solution more precisely without changing the architectural allocation (IM). Once DP is revised with explicit interface contracts and algorithmic specifications, sub-level decomposition can proceed with clear acceptance criteria for each sub-FR.

assumptions: Network Link module is capable of exposing packet loss rate, FEC overhead, latency, jitter, and link state metrics with sufficient granularity and update frequency to support real-time adaptive tuning.
Customer-defined packet loss thresholds are stable during a teleoperation session and can be provided via a single authoritative configuration source (not scattered across multiple modules).

SafetyAlert mechanism is independent of the primary teleoperation network link and provides reliable delivery under degraded network conditions (e.g., out-of-band channel, dedicated safety link, or local alert).

Safety Services module is capable of receiving SafetyAlert notifications and responding with appropriate safety-critical actions without imposing conflicting constraints on Teleoperation network health logic.
Operator interface is capable of displaying network health status, degradation alerts, and operational constraint feedback (e.g., 'telemetry-only mode', 'reduced command frequency') in real-time.
FEC and adaptive redundancy tuning algorithms can be implemented with deterministic behavior and bounded convergence time suitable for safety-critical teleoperation.
open_risks: SafetyAlert coordination dependency: If Safety Services enforces independent safety thresholds that conflict with network-induced operational constraints, constraint enforcement logic may become diffused. Mitigation: establish clear SafetyAlert contract defining ownership of threshold enforcement decision logic before implementation.

Threshold configuration diffusion: If customer-defined packet loss thresholds are managed outside Teleoperation and accessed via multiple interfaces, threshold logic may scatter across modules. Mitigation: define single authoritative source for threshold configuration and expose via explicit interface.

Network Link interface insufficiency: If Network Link does not provide sufficient visibility into packet loss metrics and link health telemetry, Teleoperation cannot implement continuous monitoring, forcing diffusion of health monitoring responsibility. Mitigation: explicitly specify Network Link interface contract before implementation.

SafetyAlert delivery circularity: If SafetyAlert is routed through the same unreliable primary teleoperation link, alert delivery cannot be guaranteed under degraded conditions, creating circular reliability dependency. Mitigation: confirm SafetyAlert uses independent transport mechanism.

Adaptive tuning unpredictability: If FEC and redundancy tuning algorithm is not formally specified, implementation may exhibit unpredictable behavior under varying network conditions, complicating verification and operator trust. Mitigation: document algorithm with decision criteria, bounds, and convergence behavior.

Operator constraint awareness: If operational constraints (e.g., degraded mode, reduced command frequency) are not communicated to operator or Tasking module, operator may issue unsafe commands under degraded conditions. Mitigation: define interface for constraint feedback and ensure operator situational awareness.
evidence_needed: Formalized Network Link interface contract specifying: packet loss rate metric definition and measurement method; FEC overhead metric; link quality metrics (latency, jitter); link state enumeration (healthy/degraded/failed); telemetry schema and update frequency; and reliability guarantees for metric delivery.

Threshold configuration interface specification: clarify whether thresholds are static (loaded at initialization), dynamic (via Tasking interface), or managed by separate Configuration module; document interface contract and access patterns.

SafetyAlert interface contract: define alert types (e.g., 'network_degraded', 'safety_critical_delivery_at_risk'); specify threshold criteria for each alert type; clarify Teleoperation ownership of network health decision logic and Safety Services ownership of safety-critical response logic; document alert delivery mechanism and reliability guarantees.

SafetyAlert transport mechanism confirmation: verify that SafetyAlert uses independent channel (out-of-band, dedicated safety link, or local alert) and document reliability guarantees under degraded network conditions.

Operational constraint feedback interface: define how Teleoperation communicates network-induced constraints (e.g., 'telemetry-only mode', 'reduced command frequency') to operator or Tasking module; specify interface contract and update frequency.

FEC and adaptive redundancy tuning algorithm specification: document decision criteria for tuning FEC parameters and redundancy level based on observed packet loss rate; specify tuning bounds, convergence behavior, and verification approach; include pseudocode or formal specification suitable for implementation and testing.
